mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensively covers the theory of chemical bonding (Section D) including nonquantum, quantum-mechanical, and other bonding effects such as hydrogen, metallic, and coordinate bonds. Also addresses classification of compounds by bond type (E.2: ionic, covalent, coordination).

As the parent discipline article, Chemistry covers the systems of classification of chemical compounds (E) and provides broad context for organic and inorganic compound families (G), including reaction types and subdisciplines relevant to the full section.

Directly covers the major groups of organic compounds, their nomenclature, properties, synthesis, and reactions (G.1.a–G.1.u), preparation and purification of organic compounds (G.2), and their physical properties (G.3).

Defines and classifies chemical compounds by elemental composition, bond type (ionic, covalent, coordination), and physical state, directly addressing outline sections E.1 through E.4 on the systems of classification of chemical substances.

Covers the theory of molecular structure (A), including molecular bonds and three-dimensional shapes (A.3) determined by VSEPR and spectroscopy, and the relation of molecular structure to properties of bulk matter (A.5).
Covers atomic and molecular orbital concepts (D.2.a) and the quantum-mechanical treatment of chemical bonding (D.2), as valence electrons govern bond formation and molecular orbital theory.

Directly covers the structural classification of inorganic compounds including coordination compounds (F.2.c), methods of preparation (F.3), and reactions of inorganic compounds including acid-base, substitution, and redox reactions (F.4).

Covers experimental and instrumental procedures for determining molecular structures (B.3 spectrochemical methods), molecular spectra (C), and the quantum-mechanical theory of chemical bonding (D.2) as core subfields of physical chemistry.

As the fundamental unit underlying molecular structure, the atom article covers atomic orbitals and electron configurations (D.2.a) and the quantum-mechanical basis for how atoms combine to form molecules, foundational to Section A's theory of molecular structure.

Carbon's unique tetravalency and bonding versatility are the foundation of the organic compounds section (G); specifically covers hydrocarbons (G.1.a) and, through its allotropes, organic polymers including fullerenes and graphene (G.1.u).

Covers the electromagnetic spectrum underlying spectrochemical analytical methods (B.3.a: microwave, infrared, UV spectroscopy) and the types of molecular spectra (C.2), which are central to identifying molecular structure.

DNA is the primary exemplar of the nucleic acids subcategory of organic compounds (G.1.r: nucleic acids — DNA and RNA), covering its double-helical polymer structure of nucleotide monomers and associated chemistry.

RNA is the second nucleic acid covered explicitly in outline path G.1.r (nucleic acids: DNA and RNA), with its distinct single-stranded polymer structure, ribose backbone, and diverse functional roles as molecular species.

Directly corresponds to outline path G.1.m (carbohydrates), covering monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides with their nomenclature, structure, chemical and physical properties, and biosynthetic occurrence.
Directly corresponds to outline path G.1.n (amino acids, proteins, and peptides), covering the polymer structure, sequence-function relationships, and chemical properties of protein macromolecules.

Natural rubber is a biopolymer of isoprene (isoprenoid, G.1.o) and simultaneously an organic polymer (G.1.u), directly illustrating both subcategories with its polyisoprene structure, vulcanization chemistry, and properties.

Silicon corresponds directly to the inorganic polymers path (F.2.f: silicate structures and inorganic polymers) and organic silicon compounds (G.1.j: organosilicon chemistry including silicones), covering both inorganic and organometallic aspects.

Oxygen directly relates to inorganic oxides, anhydrides, acids, and bases (F.2.b) and to classification by chemical reactivity as an oxidizing agent (E.3), providing specific coverage of two otherwise uncovered paths.

Carbon dioxide is a canonical example of an inorganic oxide and anhydride (F.2.b), with its linear triatomic molecular structure and acid-forming properties (carbonic acid) illustrating structural classification of inorganic compounds.
Directly covers classification of compounds by chemical reactivity as acids, bases, and salts (E.3) and acid-base reactions of inorganic compounds (F.4), providing coverage for both outline paths.

Covers classification of chemical substances by physical state — gas, liquid, solid (E.4) — and discusses pure versus natural and synthetic compounds, addressing E.5 (by origin: natural and synthetic) among uncovered paths.

Water illustrates hydrogen bonding as a major bonding effect (D.3) and its anomalous density and surface properties demonstrate how molecular structure influences physical properties of bulk matter (A.5.a).

Lavoisier's Chemical Revolution established early concepts of elemental composition and molecular structure (A.1: early concepts of molecular structure), including chemical nomenclature that persists in naming inorganic compounds (F.1).

Biochemistry encompasses the major classes of biomolecular organic compounds covered in G.1.m–G.1.r (carbohydrates, proteins, nucleotides, nucleic acids), providing broader disciplinary context for these organic compound families.

Natural gas is composed primarily of methane and higher alkanes — aliphatic hydrocarbons — directly corresponding to outline path G.1.a (hydrocarbons: aliphatic and aromatic), providing a real-world exemplar of this organic compound class.

Covers the factors affecting synthetic paths and reaction conditions in the synthesis of chemical compounds (H and H.1), as reaction types (decomposition, substitution, addition) are integral to understanding compound preparation.
